module_mood_contract: Satisfied compounding — it runs while I sleep. Student leaves feeling the memory is real, alive, and theirs — not a demo artifact.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Forcing functions live in prompts, not body prose.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 4 body, line 168: 'Before you start approving or rejecting, ask Claude one more thing: which of the six proposals does it have lowest confidence in? Force a tiering before you decide.' — this forcing function is stated in body prose AFTER the prompt block, but the Phase 4 prompt itself already includes 'name the one you are LEAST confident about and say why you included it rather than a more serious gap.' The body prose duplicates a forcing function that already lives correctly in the prompt, creating a confusing redundancy: the persona read the prompt first, completed it correctly, then read body prose telling her to do what she already did. Speed-runners skipping body prose miss nothing; students who read body first are confused about whether they need an extra step. The fix is to cut the body duplication — the forcing function already lives where it belongs (in the prompt).
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Remove the body sentence at line 168 ('Before you start approving or rejecting...') — the forcing function already fires from inside the prompt; the body is a redundant duplicate that confuses students who read it.

rules_evaluated.evidence: M2 mood contract 'satisfied compounding — it runs while I sleep' is honored throughout. Phase 2 close: 'Quietly, you just made a reusable capability. Same agent, next week's task.' Phase 3 close: 'Chat literally cannot do this.' Close section: 'the move you'll repeat at your desk on Monday, and every Monday after.' All three beats name the repeating, compounding nature without resolving into awe (M8's territory). Trace mood scores: Phase 1=8, Phase 2=9, Phase 3=8, Phase 4=7 (facilitator-recoverable), Close=9.

No punitive framing anywhere. The Phase 1 'soft-pages.md' list names pages to sharpen, not mistakes the student made. Phase 4 reject-vs-approve framing ('Some will be right. Some will miss. Reject those.') is neutral-epistemic, not shame-adjacent.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Lead with the discipline, not the failure mode.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 1 opens: 'A memory is only as good as what goes into it.' (curation discipline). Phase 2: 'A library without a librarian is a cost. Give it one.' (agent-as-discipline). Phase 3: 'A dumb knowledge base grows. A compounding one sharpens.' All disciplinary, not failure-mode.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Strategy-fidelity check — no front-running downstream teaching moments.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 1 Beat 2 explicitly defers data-classification nuance: 'Module 4 is the proper conversation about classification, data boundaries, and what an agent should never see.' Does not teach M4's topic; names it as deferred. Agent creation lands at M2 as designed (not M3/M4 territory). Compounding pattern introduced here but not over-named — M3 ownership of deeper compounding mechanics is not pre-empted.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Walk-away test — does the student leave the chair while the agent works?
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 1 Beat 2 ('Agent does the heavy lifting; you don't copy-paste') and Beat 3 auto-mode approval both create walk-away moments. Phase 3 integration runs without the student copying content. Close run through the agent reads output, not generates it. Agentic distance is ramped correctly for M2.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Student agency — adults decide. No forced pauses, no compulsion.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 3 and Close sections invite student action without compelling: 'Pick one source that fills a gap' (student chooses). Close: 'Ask your memory the hardest open question on your challenge right now' — no mandated content. Phase 4 approval sequence: 'Some will be right. Some will miss. Reject those.' — student decides, not scripted.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Delegation boundaries — student brings, Claude screens.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 1 curation: Claude surveys connectors and asks about the student's world (Claude doesn't pick sources unilaterally — it proposes a plan the student pushes back on). Phase 4: Claude proposes fixes, student approves/rejects. The judgment about what to include and what to reject stays with the student at every decision point.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Classroom is the default delivery mode — conversation pause beats authored in.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Natural pause beats exist at: (1) Beat 1/Beat 2 transition (student reviews curation plan before ingest runs); (2) plan-mode approval point in Beat 3 (designed pause with option 4 feedback loop); (3) Phase 2/agent-use transition (fresh message = new turn = natural pause); (4) Phase 3 push-back beat (student challenges Claude's 'sharper' claim); (5) Phase 4 approve/reject sequence. Rule 27 caveat: pause beats are trainer-side cues in the surrounding prose, not student-commanded share-with-person-next-to-you beats. All beats here are structurally natural rather than commanded. Story rule 27 classroom-pause-beat shape attempted with 'compare with person next to you' form was reverted (check_student_facing rule 26 hard violation) — current state is approve-with-todo.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Classroom-pause-beat authored-in beats pass via natural exercise structure. The 'compare with neighbour' shape is architecturally blocked by check_student_facing rule 26 — pending /refresh resolution. Current state is approve.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Meta-frame lectures are closers, not openers.
rules_evaluated.evidence: This is an exercise file, not a lecture. No lecture placement decision to evaluate.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Offered structures need forced-engagement mechanic — plan-mode plans get rubber-stamped.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Beat 3 plan-mode approval block (lines 63-68) explicitly names options 1/4 with forced-engagement path: 'option 4 (Tell Claude what to change) and type one sentence of feedback; Claude rewrites the plan.' The two rubber-stamp paths (options 2/3) are named and contextually dismissed. Phase 4 approval: 'Some will be right. Some will miss. Reject those.' — expectation of rejection baked in, not bare approval gate.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Self-report inflation — verify at artifact, not summary; over-flag or fresh-session for self-audit.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 3 body (line 156-157): 'Push back if a claim got sharper but the top didn't actually change.' — student is directed to verify at the artifact (the top of the page) not trust Claude's self-report. Phase 1 self-audit prompt (lines 96-99): 'Assume at least one of the three has a generic top claim' — over-flag framing applied. Both mitigations present.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Measure silent failure — frictionless is not good.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 4 teach moment is structurally designed to produce friction: Claude proposes fixes the student may disagree with, and 'Reject those' is explicitly named. The Phase 1 curation beat creates friction through the one-at-a-time question sequence and the student pushback loop. Silent failure (student approves all 6 proposals without reading) is partially guarded by the forcing-function in P4 prompt ('name the one you are LEAST confident about') — though body duplication slightly dilutes the force.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Artifact-realism check — named tools, skills, paths must exist.
rules_evaluated.evidence: sources/, memory/, agents/ folders named as pre-existing from prework (line 9). challenge.md from opener exercise. Plan mode is a real Claude Code feature. No phantom skills or paths invoked. The Prework connector requirement (Confluence + OneDrive) is appropriately deferred to prework setup, not assumed in-exercise.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Question-dump default — one-at-a-time must be structurally enforced.
rules_evaluated.evidence: P1 Beat 1 prompt (line 31): 'Ask one question at a time. Wait for my answer before asking the next. Do not show me the question list.' — one-at-a-time structurally enforced with both positive instruction and list-suppression. P2 agent-creation prompt (line 115): 'Ask me these one at a time. Wait for my answer before asking the next. Do not show me the question list.' — same pattern applied. Both prompts pass the structural enforcement test.

P3 (Phase 3 integrate prompt) uses open-hook pattern: last line is 'New source:' with colon, no bracket placeholder. Student pastes link or path after the colon. All other prompts use scrollback references ('the curation plan we just agreed', 'the agent file you just created') or prompt Claude to ask. No [BRACKET] placeholders anywhere in fenced blocks.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Append-vs-integrate — name the integration mode on regeneration with new source.
rules_evaluated.evidence: P3 integrate prompt (lines 148-150): 'Integrate its claims into existing pages (sharpen, don't append). Drop any claim the source contradicts. For new topics, add pages in the existing shape. Rewrite tops in place. Replace old framing; don't preserve it above a new section.' — integration mode explicitly named on both dimensions (integrate not append, overwrite not preserve).

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Strategic themes recur in Key Concepts.
rules_evaluated.evidence: This is an exercise file, not a module file. Key Concepts section lives in the module file that includes this exercise. Not evaluated here.

stealing_the_mood: Phase 4 (score 7): Body prose at line 168 tells the student to ask Claude which proposal it is least confident in — but the Phase 4 prompt already ends with that exact instruction. The redundancy reads as distrust of the prompt and creates a false sense that an extra step is required. Facilitator-recoverable (Teacher Claude narrates 'you just rejected two proposals — that's the discipline') but structurally fixable by removing the body duplication.
